feedback.text: Dear Sir or Madam, In response to the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act, I would like to raise several critical concerns regarding unfair practices in the digital gaming sector and related services. These issues require urgent regulatory attention: 1. Planned Obsolescence in Online Games A growing number of game publishers are shutting down online servers, rendering purchased games completely unusable. This practice constitutes a form of planned obsolescence, which contradicts the principles of product durability outlined in Directive 2005/29/EC on Unfair Commercial Practices and recent EU initiatives promoting sustainable consumption. Consumers who purchase a game should retain access to it for a reasonable period, regardless of the publishers decision to discontinue support. 2. Misleading Use of the Buy Button The Buy button in digital storefronts often refers not to the purchase of a product, but to a temporary license, misleading consumers about the nature of the transaction. This constitutes false advertising and violates the transparency requirements of Directive 2011/83/EU on Consumer Rights. Consumers must be clearly informed whether they are acquiring ownership or merely a time-limited license. This practice also conflicts with Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 (P2B Regulation), which mandates transparency in commercial terms. 3. EULA Displayed Post-Purchase and Unilateral Changes End User License Agreements (EULAs) are frequently presented only after the purchase and can be unilaterally modified by the publisher. Consumers are forced to accept these changes or lose access to previously purchased content. This violates the principle of contractual fairness and is contrary to Directive 93/13/EEC on Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts, which prohibits clauses allowing unilateral changes without consumer consent. 4. Payment Processors as Gatekeepers and Censors Payment processors such as Mastercard and Visa increasingly act as gatekeepers, deciding what consumers can buy and from whom, effectively enforcing economic censorship. These practices undermine economic freedom and may violate Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 (Digital Markets Act), which aims to curb the abuse of dominant positions by core platform services. I hope these concerns will be taken into account in the further development of the Digital Fairness Act. Consumers deserve transparency, durability of digital products, and protection from arbitrary corporate decisions. Sincerely, Rafał Pasternak
